transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Leo. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, thanks for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my job lately. I recently got a bonus for my performance, which should feel good, right? But when I shared the news with my parents, it didn’t go as I expected. They asked if I was looking for a new job with better prospects, and it kind of threw me off. I don’t know, it just made me feel... anxious, I guess. Like maybe they don’t see my accomplishments the way I do.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’ve been reflecting on your job and your recent bonus, which should be a positive achievement. However, when you shared that news with your parents, their response left you feeling anxious and uncertain about how they perceive your accomplishments. Is that right? What do you think it is about their reaction that affected you so deeply?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, that’s pretty much it. I mean, I thought they’d be happy for me, but instead, it felt like they were questioning my choices. It’s like, I worked hard for this, and instead of celebrating, they’re pushing me to think about something else. I guess it makes me feel vulnerable, like I’m not doing enough or that they might judge me for where I am in my career. I don’t know, it just made me want to pull back and not share anything else with them.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really disappointing to feel like your hard work isn’t being recognized in the way you hoped. Their questioning seems to have made you feel vulnerable and perhaps even judged, which can be really tough. It’s understandable that you’d want to pull back from sharing more with them after that experience. Can you tell me more about what it feels like to think that they might judge you for where you are in your career?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s just... I don’t know, it feels like there’s this pressure to always be moving forward, to have the next big thing lined up. When they ask about looking for better prospects, it makes me feel like what I’ve achieved isn’t enough. It’s like I’m constantly comparing myself to some ideal of success that I’m not sure I can reach. I guess I worry that they’ll think less of me if I’m not always striving for more. It’s frustrating because I want to feel proud of what I’ve done, but their reaction just makes me second-guess everything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a lot of pressure you feel to continuously achieve more, and that your parents’ expectations contribute to that. It’s frustrating to feel like your accomplishments aren’t enough, especially when you want to take pride in what you’ve achieved. The comparison to an ideal of success can be really overwhelming. How do you usually cope with those feelings of frustration and self-doubt when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, I’m not sure I cope with them very well. I tend to just... withdraw. Like after that conversation with my parents, I didn’t call them for a couple of weeks. I thought maybe if I didn’t talk about my job, I wouldn’t have to deal with those feelings. But then it just builds up, you know? I try to focus on my work and keep my head down, but it’s hard to shake off that nagging feeling of not being good enough. Sometimes I just feel stuck in this cycle of wanting to share my successes but being too afraid of how they’ll react.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like withdrawing feels like a way to protect yourself from those uncomfortable feelings, but it also leads to a buildup of emotions that can be hard to manage. It’s understandable to want to share your successes, yet feeling afraid of their reaction creates a difficult cycle for you. That tension between wanting connection and fearing judgment can be really challenging. When you think about breaking that cycle, what do you imagine would help you feel more comfortable sharing your achievements with them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe if I could find a way to communicate my feelings without feeling so vulnerable, that might help. Like, if I could express how much their support means to me, maybe they’d understand better. But it’s hard to find the right words when I’m feeling anxious. I guess I just wish I could feel more confident in my own achievements, so their questions wouldn’t affect me as much. It would be nice to have a conversation where I felt like I could share openly without worrying about being judged. But I don’t know how to get there.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re looking for a way to express your feelings and needs in a way that feels safe and confident. Wanting to communicate how much their support means to you is a great step, but I can see how anxiety makes it challenging to find the right words. It’s understandable to wish for a conversation where you can share openly without fear of judgment. What do you think might help you build that confidence in your achievements, so you feel more secure in those conversations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to remind myself of my accomplishments more often, like keeping a list or something. Sometimes I just forget what I’ve done because I’m so focused on what’s next. Maybe talking through my feelings with someone else, like a friend or even here, could help me process things better. If I could practice expressing my thoughts and feelings in a safe space, it might make it easier when I talk to my parents. I guess I just need to find a way to validate myself before I seek that validation from them.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach. Keeping a list of your accomplishments could serve as a great reminder of your hard work and progress, helping you to focus on what you’ve achieved rather than just what’s next. Practicing expressing your thoughts and feelings in a safe space, like here or with a friend, could definitely help you feel more prepared for those conversations with your parents. It’s insightful that you recognize the importance of self-validation before seeking it from others. How do you feel about starting with that self-validation? What might be the first step you’d like to take?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the first step could be to actually sit down and write out that list of accomplishments. Just taking the time to reflect on what I’ve achieved, big or small, might help me see things from a different perspective. I could also try to set aside some time to talk about my feelings with a friend, maybe even practice how I’d want to share things with my parents. It feels a bit daunting, but I think it could be a good way to start building that confidence. I just hope I can stick with it and not let the anxiety get in the way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Leo. Taking the time to reflect on your accomplishments and talking through your feelings with a friend could really help shift your perspective and build your confidence. It’s completely normal to feel a bit daunted by the process, especially when anxiety is involved. Remember, it’s okay to take it one step at a time. How do you think you might handle those moments of anxiety when they come up as you start this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I might try to remind myself that it’s okay to feel anxious and that it doesn’t define my worth or my accomplishments. Maybe I could use some breathing exercises or grounding techniques to help calm myself down in those moments. I could also try to challenge those negative thoughts that pop up, like reminding myself that I’ve worked hard and deserve to feel proud. It might help to have a mantra or something to repeat to myself when I start feeling overwhelmed. I guess it’s about finding ways to manage the anxiety while still pushing myself to take those steps forward.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and proactive approach. Acknowledging that anxiety is okay and doesn’t define your worth is a powerful mindset. Using breathing exercises and grounding techniques can definitely help in those moments of overwhelm. Challenging negative thoughts and having a mantra to remind yourself of your hard work and achievements can also be very effective. It seems like you’re building a toolkit for yourself to manage anxiety while still moving forward. How do you feel about the idea of creating that mantra? What might it look or sound like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think creating a mantra could be really helpful. It would be something I could hold onto when I start feeling anxious or doubt myself. Maybe something like, “I am enough, and my achievements matter.” Or even, “I’ve worked hard, and I deserve to celebrate my successes.” I want it to be simple but powerful enough to remind me of my worth. I guess I could try saying it to myself when I’m feeling overwhelmed or before I talk to my parents. Having that reminder might help me feel more grounded and confident in those moments.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are beautiful mantras, Leo. They carry a strong message of self-acceptance and recognition of your hard work. Having something simple yet powerful to remind you of your worth can be really grounding, especially in moments of anxiety. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success by preparing these affirmations for when you need them most. How do you feel about incorporating these mantras into your daily routine? Would you want to practice saying them regularly, or perhaps write them down somewhere visible?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Incorporating them into my daily routine sounds like a good idea. I think I’d like to write them down and maybe put them somewhere I can see them every day, like on my desk or even on my bathroom mirror. That way, I can start my day with that reminder and keep it in mind throughout the day. I could also set a specific time to practice saying them, like in the morning or before I go to bed. It might help reinforce those positive thoughts and make them feel more natural when I need to rely on them. I’m hoping that with time, it’ll help shift how I view myself and my accomplishments.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful plan, Leo. Writing your mantras down and placing them where you can see them daily is a great way to keep those positive reminders front and center. Setting specific times to practice saying them can help reinforce those affirmations and make them feel more natural over time. It’s encouraging to hear that you’re hopeful about shifting how you view yourself and your accomplishments. As you start this practice, how do you think you’ll know that it’s making a difference for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll know it’s making a difference if I start to feel a bit more confident in sharing my achievements, even if it’s just a small step. If I notice that I’m less anxious when I think about talking to my parents or that I’m able to celebrate my successes without second-guessing myself, that would be a good sign. I’d also like to see if I feel more at ease with my own accomplishments, like being able to acknowledge them without feeling guilty or unworthy. If I can catch myself challenging those negative thoughts more easily, that would be a positive change too. I guess it’ll be about feeling more grounded and secure in who I am and what I’ve done.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are insightful indicators of progress, Leo. Feeling more confident in sharing your achievements, experiencing less anxiety around conversations with your parents, and being able to celebrate your successes without guilt are all meaningful signs of growth. It’s great that you’re focusing on feeling more grounded and secure in your identity and accomplishments. As you embark on this journey, remember to be patient with yourself and acknowledge even the small steps forward. Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss today as you think about this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind today. I appreciate having the space to talk about it. I guess I just want to make sure I stay committed to this process and don’t fall back into old habits of withdrawing or doubting myself. Maybe I could set some reminders for myself to check in on how I’m feeling as I start this new practice. It helps to know that I have a plan and some tools to work with. Thank you for guiding me through this; it’s been really helpful.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful, Leo. It sounds like you’re taking some thoughtful steps to stay committed to your process, and setting reminders to check in on your feelings is a great idea. Having a plan and tools to work with can really support you as you navigate this journey. Remember, it’s okay to have ups and downs along the way; what matters is that you’re actively working towards your goals. If you ever want to talk again or need support as you implement these practices, I’m here for you. Take care as you move forward!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. It feels good to have a plan and know that I can reach out if I need support. I’ll definitely keep working on these steps and check in with myself regularly. It’s nice to have someone to talk to about all this. Take care, and I’ll see you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Leo. I’m glad you feel supported and have a plan in place. Remember, you’re not alone in this process, and it’s great that you’re committed to checking in with yourself. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. Wishing you all the best as you move forward!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate your support and encouragement. I’ll keep working on everything we discussed. Looking forward to our next conversation too. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Leo! I’m really glad to have been able to support you today. Best of luck as you work on everything we discussed. I look forward to hearing how things go in our next conversation. Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate it. I’ll do my best to keep moving forward and look forward to sharing my progress next time. Take care!
